questions.stem: A laboring client receives an epidural and 5 minutes later becomes pale and dizzy. Blood pressure is 78/42 mmHg and the fetal heart rate shows recurrent late decelerations. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Reposition the client and increase the IV fluid rate.
Encourage the client to push with the next contraction.
Lower the head of the bed and leave the client flat.
Document an expected epidural response and reassess later.

questions.rationale: Maternal hypotension after epidural analgesia can reduce uteroplacental perfusion and trigger late decelerations. The nurse should reposition the client and support circulating volume immediately. Delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk.

distractorRationales.b: "Encourage the client to push with the next contraction" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "Delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before diagnostic reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Reposition the client and increase the IV fluid rate" fits the safer response to the cue "Blood pressure is 78/42 mmHg and the fetal heart rate shows recurrent late decelerations".
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Blood pressure is 78/42 mmHg and the fetal heart rate shows recurrent late decelerations" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Maternal hypotension after epidural should be corrected quickly to restore fetal perfusion.

questions.stem: A client with acute kidney injury has a potassium level of 6.8 mEq/L and peaked T waves on the cardiac monitor. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Prepare to administer IV calcium gluconate.
Encourage the client to increase oral fluids.
Place the client on a potassium-restricted diet.
Repeat the potassium level in 4 hours.

questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes places the client at immediate risk for lethal dysrhythmias. IV calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane while other therapies work to lower serum potassium. Dietary teaching and delayed reassessment do not address the urgent electrical instability.

distractorRationales.b: "Encourage the client to increase oral fluids" misses the bedside risk. The cue "A client with acute kidney injury has a potassium level of 6" points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: "Place the client on a potassium-restricted diet" misses the bedside risk. The cue "A client with acute kidney injury has a potassium level of 6" points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: "Repeat the potassium level in 4 hours" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "A client with acute kidney injury has a potassium level of 6" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.

questions.takeaway: Hyperkalemia plus peaked T waves is a cardiac emergency: stabilize the myocardium first.

questions.stem: A client taking lithium for bipolar disorder has vomiting, coarse hand tremors, and increasing confusion after several days of diarrhea. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Hold the lithium and notify the provider.
Give the lithium with crackers to reduce stomach upset.
Teach the client to double the next dose.
Encourage the client to restrict sodium intake further.

questions.rationale: Vomiting, worsening tremor, and confusion after fluid loss suggest lithium toxicity. The nurse should stop further doses and notify the provider for prompt evaluation. Dehydration and sodium loss can raise lithium levels, so more restriction or additional dosing would be unsafe.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of medication-safety intervention. The stem cue "A client taking lithium for bipolar disorder has vomiting, coarse hand tremors" makes medication toxicity the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave medication toxicity untreated because it uses teaching before the physiologic risk is controlled. "Hold the lithium and notify the provider" fits the safer response to the cue "confusion after fluid loss suggest lithium toxicity".
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Encourage the client to restrict sodium intake further" moves care toward routine care before medication-safety intervention.

questions.takeaway: GI loss plus confusion and coarse tremor on lithium should be treated as toxicity until proven otherwise.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: The RN is assigning care for a group of postoperative clients. Which task is appropriate to delegate to an experienced LPN/LVN?

options.text: Reinforce teaching already provided about incentive spirometry.
Perform the initial assessment of a client returning from PACU.
Develop the nursing care plan for a new admission.
Interpret a change in the client's telemetry rhythm.

questions.rationale: An experienced LPN or LVN may reinforce established teaching and perform focused care for stable clients, depending on policy. The initial postoperative assessment, care planning, and rhythm interpretation require RN-level assessment and clinical judgment. Safe delegation depends on stability, predictability, and the need for judgment.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "Perform the initial assessment of a client returning from PACU" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "Develop the nursing care plan for a new admission" moves care toward routine care before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "Safe delegation depends on stability, predictability" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Delegate stable, predictable care and reinforcement to the LPN or LVN, not initial assessment or interpretation.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: In the postanesthesia care unit, a client develops rising end-tidal CO2, muscle rigidity, and a rapidly increasing temperature. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Prepare to administer dantrolene and call the rapid response team.
Cover the client with warm blankets to prevent shivering.
Encourage the client to cough and deep-breathe.
Delay action until laboratory confirmation is available.

questions.rationale: Rising end-tidal CO2, rigidity, and hyperthermia after anesthesia suggest malignant hyperthermia, a life-threatening emergency. Dantrolene and immediate escalation are critical. Waiting for lab confirmation or focusing on comfort measures delays definitive treatment.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "Cover the client with warm blankets to prevent shivering" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "Encourage the client to cough and deep-breathe" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Prepare to administer dantrolene and call the rapid response team" fits the safer response to the cue "hyperthermia after anesthesia suggest malignant hyperthermia, a life-threatening emergency".

questions.takeaway: Suspected malignant hyperthermia demands immediate dantrolene and emergency escalation.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client is admitted with suspected bacterial meningitis. Which infection-control measure should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Initiate droplet precautions.
Place the client in airborne isolation.
Use reverse isolation to protect the client.
Delay precautions until a lumbar puncture confirms the diagnosis.

questions.rationale: Suspected bacterial meningitis requires prompt droplet precautions to reduce transmission risk. The nurse should not wait for confirmation before initiating appropriate isolation. Airborne or reverse isolation do not match the primary transmission risk for typical bacterial meningitis.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "Airborne or reverse isolation do not match the primary transmission risk for typical bacterial meningitis" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Initiate droplet precautions" fits the safer response to the cue "Airborne or reverse isolation do not match the primary transmission risk for typical bacterial meningitis".
distractorRationales.d: "Delay precautions until a lumbar puncture confirms the diagnosis" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Suspected bacterial meningitis requires prompt droplet precautions to reduce transmission risk" points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.

questions.takeaway: Start droplet precautions right away when bacterial meningitis is suspected.

questions.stem: A client receiving chemotherapy has an absolute neutrophil count of 400/mm3. Which nursing action is most appropriate?

options.text: Avoid fresh flowers and raw fruits brought from outside.
Take a rectal temperature every 4 hours.
Limit oral care to once each shift to avoid bleeding.
Encourage the client to visit the crowded day room to reduce isolation.

questions.rationale: An absolute neutrophil count of 400/mm3 indicates severe neutropenia and a high risk for infection. Reducing exposure to environmental organisms, including flowers and unwashed fresh produce, is appropriate. Rectal temperatures and unnecessary crowd exposure increase infection risk rather than protecting the client.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "A client receiving chemotherapy has an absolute neutrophil count of 400/mm3" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: "Limit oral care to once each shift to avoid bleeding" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Rectal temperatures and unnecessary crowd exposure increase infection risk rather than protecting the client" points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.d: "Encourage the client to visit the crowded day room to reduce isolation" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Rectal temperatures and unnecessary crowd exposure increase infection risk rather than protecting the client" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: With severe neutropenia, the nurse should lower exposure to infection sources rather than increase invasive or crowd contact.

questions.stem: A newborn is 18 hours old and is noted to have yellow discoloration of the sclera and chest. Which response by the nurse is best?

options.text: Report the early jaundice because jaundice in the first 24 hours is concerning.
Reassure the parent that all newborn jaundice is expected.
Encourage the parent to place the newborn in direct sunlight.
Wait until the newborn is 48 hours old before documenting the finding.

questions.rationale: Jaundice appearing in the first 24 hours of life is not considered normal transitional jaundice and should be reported for prompt evaluation. Early jaundice can signal hemolysis or other pathologic causes. Reassurance or delay risks missing rising bilirubin levels that may need treatment.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. "Report the early jaundice because jaundice in the first 24 hours is concerning" fits the safer response to the cue "Jaundice in the first 24 hours is a report-now finding in the newborn".
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Reassurance or delay risks missing rising bilirubin levels that may need treatment" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Report the early jaundice because jaundice in the first 24 hours is concerning" fits the safer response to the cue "Reassurance or delay risks missing rising bilirubin levels that may need treatment".

questions.takeaway: Jaundice in the first 24 hours is a report-now finding in the newborn.

questions.stem: Two days after a cesarean birth, a client has uterine tenderness, foul-smelling lochia, and a temperature of 101.8 F (38.8 C). Which complication should the nurse suspect?

options.text: Endometritis.
Uterine inversion.
Postpartum blues.
Expected afterpains.

questions.rationale: Fever, uterine tenderness, and foul-smelling lochia after birth are classic findings of endometritis. The cesarean birth increases infection risk, making this complication especially important to recognize quickly. Uterine inversion is an acute obstetric emergency with a very different presentation, and postpartum blues do not cause fever or uterine pain.

distractorRationales.b: "Uterine inversion" misses the bedside risk. The cue "postpartum blues do not cause fever or uterine pain" points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.c: "Postpartum blues" misses the bedside risk. The cue "postpartum blues do not cause fever or uterine pain" points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.d: "Expected afterpains" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "postpartum blues do not cause fever or uterine pain" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Postpartum fever with uterine tenderness and foul lochia points to endometritis, not routine recovery discomfort.

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: "The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision" raises concern for clinical safety risk, so bedside safety action is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency" suggests clinical safety risk, so "These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well" would not correct the active problem. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids" fits the safer response to the cue "A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg".
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "Teach the client about low-sodium foods" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise" suggests oxygenation or ventilation, so "Obtain a full allergy history before intervening" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: "Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine" fits the safer response to the cue "A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives".

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.
